text:
Admission Date:  2010-08-21              Discharge Date:   1989-05-23

Date of Birth:  2002-05-14             Sex:   F

Service: PSYCHIATRY

Allergies:
Ambien

Attending:William
Chief Complaint:
"I came to the hospital b/c my magnesium was low and I stopped
taking insulin"

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

History of Present Illness:
34 y/o F with h/o IDDM, mitochondrial myopathy, legal blindness
admitted to SICU today for DKA in the setting of insulin
non-adherence. Primary team reports increasing social isolation
and that pt stated stopped taking insulin because she "didn't
care." Psychiatry consulted for a safety evaluation.

History of Present Illness: The patient reports that she
presented to the ED today for pains in her legs and concern that
her magnesium level was low. She states that she stopped taking
her insulin 1-2 months ago she "didn't care" and she declines to
elaborate further.

The patient reports that she started taking prozac prescribed by
her PCP in [**Name9 (PRE) 958**] or 05 so that "it could do what is supposed
to do." When asked to clarify, the patient says that she does
not
want to go into it now. She says that the prozac is not working
very well, and she wishes she weren't taking it. She denies
feeling depressed or anxious and she denies having suicidal
thoughts. She says she spends her days sleeping. When asked
about
other activities, she does not respond. She endorses a 36lb
unintentional weight loss since 03, which she says is partly
due to her diabetes. She says she wants to leave the hospital
and
go home  because "I am tired of being questioned." She does not
say what she would do when she got home and does not identify
anything that she is looking forward to in the future. She
describes having a few friends and is in touch with her family,
particularly her mother, by fine. She declines permission for me
to contact her mother or any of her friends.

Patient met with social work earlier today after requesting not
to have her HCP (her sister) informed of her hospitalization.
Per their note, pt was reluctant to participate in interview.
She appeared to have signs of SI and recommended psychiatry
consult.

On psychiatric ROS, she denies decreased need for sleep,
increaesed energy or grandiosity. She denies hallucinations or
paranoia.



Past Medical History:
Diabetes mellitus, type I
Hypertension
Hypomagnesemia
blindness
Gait disorder
Mitochondrial myopathy
Insomnia
Obstructive sleep apnea- on CPAP

Social History:
The patient was born and raised in [**State 760**]. She attended
Hardy school for the blind at age 16. She currently lives
alone
in an apartment in 25077 Williams Burg
Jimmyport, LA 23526. She talks to her mother sometimes
on the phone and says she has a few friends. She previously
worked for the epilepsy foundation, but is currently unemployed
and on disability. She says that she spends her days sleeping.

She was raised by her bio mother and stepfather whom she
initially reported as "fine" and later said that they are
responsible for her childhood problems.
She has a twin sister, Brandy Fisher, whom she used to be close with but
recently had a falling out - Jessica Nunez husband died suddenly in a
swimming accident and per the pt has been upset that the pt
"didn't grieve enough"

Pt enjoys 7326 Tanner Run
Port Jamie, MI 13413 books on tape especially; Ray, Cornwall,
Walters, Tracey Kelley, and she has an interest in tattoos.
Pt has a unicorn/pegasus tattoo and was hoping to get a dragon
tattoo
She has 2 cats (one is named Bagel)
She is not religious and has no plants

Family History:
Father- unknown
Kimberly Richardson Terry Roberts
George Molina mitochondrial myopathy
David Brewer colon cancer
Grandmother- breast cancer
Father- unknown
Kimberly Richardson Terry Roberts
George Molina mitochondrial myopathy
David Brewer colon cancer
Grandmother- breast cancer

Physical Exam:
VS: T 98 HR 81 BP 123/86 RR 16 SaO2 98%RA weight: 203 lbs
MENTAL STATUS EXAM
     APPEARANCE & FACIAL EXPRESSION: Adequately groomed,
overweight, caucasian woman, lying in bed wearing a t-shirt and
pants.
     BEHAVIOR: Oriented body toward examiner, cooperative. No
abnl mvts, no pmr/pma
     SPEECH: Incr speech latency. Nl R/R/T

     AFFECT: Restricted, mood incongruent.
     THOUGHT FORM: Linear

     PERCEPTIONS: No A/VH
     NEUROVEGETATIVE SYMPTOMS: Wt loss, incr sleep
     INSIGHT AND JUDGMENT: Limited/Limited. Pt cannot identify
why she stopped taking her insulin not clear if she understands
consequences.

          SENSORIUM: Alert
          ORIENTATION: person [x] place [x]  city [x] state [x]
month [x] day [x] year [x]
          ATTENTION:  Intact by DOWB

$1.50, pt responds "6"
          FUND OF KNOWLEDGE: Knows current and prior president.
Identifies president before Burke as "I know his wife's a
senator"
          PROVERB INTERPRETATION:  book/cover- "don't jusge what
what's on the outside"
          SIMILARITIES/ANALOGIES:  bike/car- wheels

General: NAD

Lungs: Clear to auscultation; no crackles or wheezes.
CV: Regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs/rubs/gallops
Abdomen: Soft, nontender, nondistended; no masses or
organomegaly.

Skin: Warm and dry, no rash or significant lesions.
Neurological:

I: Not tested

Visual fields are full to confrontation.
III, IV, VI: Extraocular movements intact bilaterally without
nystagmus.
V, VII: Facial strength and sensation intact and symmetric.
VIII: Hearing intact to voice.
IX, X: Palatal elevation symmetrical.
Zachary: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius normal bilaterally.
XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations.
*Motor- Normal bulk and tone bilaterally. No abnormal movements,
tremors. Strength full power 06-21 throughout.
*Reflexes- 2+ Biceps, Patellar

Brief Hospital Course:
PSYCHIATRIC

#) DEPRESSION/SUICIDAL THOUGHTS
The pt initially denied that her lack of taking insulin was a
suicide attempt.  She denied suicidal thoughts/intention/plans
to the team, but she also consistently repeated "I don't care"
with regards to her thoughts about life.
After being in the hospital on the psychiatric unit for a few
days, Ms. Jackson still could not reassure the team that she
would be safe in a less restrictive enviornment.  Although she
replied "no" to direct questions about suicidal ideation, she
was seen to smile during her response and stated, "I know I have
to say that to get out."  She also shared with the team that she
has felt depressed her entire life, and she has always thought
about suicide.
Index of suspcion for suicidal thoughts/intent remained high,
and the pt was informed that she would need to strategize about
safety with the team (including; agreement to f/u care, visiting
nurses into her home daily, and family involvement) to be
considered appropriate for discharge.

We adjusted her medication to help better target her depression
and associated features of; anhedonia, seclusiveness, guilt over
her brother-in-law's death and argument with her sister (see
below), pmr, and si.  We increased prozac to 80mg QD and added
Adderall 5mg QAM/2pm and she seemed to show some improvements as
evidenced by her engagement in therapy with treatment team
members and attendance at groups on the unit.

During conversations over the week of 2018-10-23, Ms. Jackson
talked more readily with the treatment team and admitted to
purposefully not taking her insulin medications as a way to
passively end her life. When we continued to have discussion
about what she would do if she were to be discharged to her
apartment, she vaguely says whether or not she lives is her
decision and she can't "predict the future" regarding her
suicidal intentions. At one point during this week, she endorses
having possible plans in mind to kill herself at her apartment.

In the ensuing weeks, she generally neither confirms or denies
suicidal ideation, oftentimes she will say "no" when asked if
she has any thoughts of harming or killing herself but will not
engage in a conversation about how her attitude may or may not
have changed on this issue during her time here.

On 1989-05-23 at approx 6:30pm patient notified nursing that she
had taken 14 tabs of her 50mg atenolol which she had apparently
been hiding in her room.  In the prior 48 hours she had become
more isolative, staying in bed and refusing to interact
significantly with the treatment team, refusing to answer
questions about her mood or suicidal thoughts.  No signs of
toxic ingestion had been seen, however, as the pt was
conversive, did mobilize without problem, and had no somatic
complaints.

#) ADJUSTMENT D/O VS. COMPLICATED BEREAVEMENT
The pt has a complicated social history that includes leaning
heavily on her sister and brother-in-law for support over the
last 10+ years after her and her sister left home to come to
Anthony Nelson for high school at the Hardy School for the Blind. Both
of them had been abused by their older brother in their
childhood, and her parents did little to intervene in that
situation, so Ms. Jackson has lost trust in all members of her
family except her sister. Brandy Fisher this year, Ms. Chang
brother-in-law died in a swimming accident, and since that time
both her and her sister have had declines in their mental
health. Her and her sister had a big disagreement and have not
been speaking for several months. As this was Ms. Chang
main support, this damaged relationship represents a significant
psychosocial stressor, along with the death of her
brother-in-law whom she had a good relationship with as well.

The family of her brother-in-law remains [**Name2 (NI) 61702**] in Ms.
Jackson and involved in her care. The brother-in-law's sister,
Stephen Watson Denise Bailey, is her health care proxy and has been in phone
communication with Ms. Jackson and her medical team. Nelson
partner also visited with the medical team in person regarding
decisions to file Section 7/8/8b and guardianship paperwork and
Ms. Chang risk of harm/suicide in the community.
Ms. Jackson refused to attempt to reach out to her sister
(didn't want to be the first one to apologize) until 2010-03-30,
when she left a phone message for her sister.
Brandy Fisher fluoxetine was titrated from 60mg daily to 80mg daily. She
was also started on adderall Hosp Perea as augmentation of
anti-depressant.

GENERAL MEDICAL CONDITIONS
#Mitochondrial disease: This is a chronic condition which
appeared to be stable throughout admission.  Patient's PCP, Friedman.
Wise was in touch with the treatment team frequently and did not
recommend any interventions.  Patient does appear to have
cardiac sequelae of mitochondrial disease and was continued on
ASA, fish oil.  Has chronic hypomagnesemia related to this as
well which often causes muscle cramping.  Was continued on
carisodoprol
#IDDM: Green was consulted. Patient was maintained on Insulin
Glargine, standing humalog and SSI Jessica Moon recs, see insulin
flow sheet
#HTN: Patient was continued on home dose of amiloride, atenolol
#Chronic Pain: continued on pregabalin, carisoprodol, ibuprofen
prn,
#Constipation: started on senna, colace, miralax, lactulose for
constipation with some effect

LEGAL STATUS
The pt was admitted on a section 12. She was initially found to
lack capacity to sign in voluntarily given her reluctance to
participate with the treatment team and denial of having a
mental illness. Given her unwillingness to plan with the team
around appropriate outpatient supports to help ensure her safety
in the community (such as VNA services, outpatient
therapy/psychiatry, weekly appointments with her PCP, Abigail Caldwell
with previous family supports such as her sister), we filed for
Section 7/8/8b and legal guardianship with the court on
2007-08-23. Court scheduled for civil commitment and Alvarez
hearing on 2006-08-19 after Ms. Jackson agrees with her attorney
to have an independent medical examination.  Legal counsel is
proceeding with application for guardianship.

RISK ASSESSMENT
Patient is being transferred to IM service for monitoring after
reported Atenolol OD.  She is considered to be a high risk of
self-harm and will need continued 1:1 observation while on
medical unit given this inpatient suicide attempt and likely
continued suicidality.  Psychiatry will follow along with
medicine team.

condition is unstable
